title: Optimization-Friendly Generic Mechanisms Without Money
speaker: Mark Braverman
abstract: Our goal is to develop a generic framework for converting modern gradient-descent based optimization algorithms into mechanisms where inputs come from self-interested agents.

proof_stages.label: Algorithm-to-mechanism reduction agenda
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames mechanism design as converting algorithms with given inputs into mechanisms that elicit strategic agents' inputs, with the target being an open-box reduction for iterative optimization heuristics without money.

proof_stages.label: VCG benchmark and fragility
proof_stages.summary: VCG is presented as the classical reduction from exact welfare optimization to truthful mechanisms with money, followed by the reasons it is unsuitable for moneyless, approximate, or heuristic large-scale settings.

proof_stages.label: Correlated-equilibrium and bandit-learning ingredients
proof_stages.summary: The talk reviews correlated equilibria as price/signal-mediated outcomes reached by low-swap-regret play, and bandits with knapsacks as a learning primitive that becomes tractable once a per-agent bang-per-buck parameter is supplied.

proof_stages.label: Equal-token scaled VCG insight
proof_stages.summary: A thought experiment with non-monetary token budgets shows that if VCG externality charges can be equalized, possibly after per-agent utility scaling, truthful reporting becomes a correlated equilibrium from equal endowments.

proof_stages.label: APEX local-pricing mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The APEX algorithm is defined as an iterative optimization loop that maximizes locally regularized scaled utilities, charges local VCG externality prices in tokens, and lets agents learn their scaling parameters by bandits with knapsacks.

proof_stages.label: Low-regret convergence implies equal-endowment correlated equilibrium
proof_stages.summary: The main equilibrium theorem states that if the APEX execution converges with low regret for the players, then the trajectory induces a correlated equilibrium in which no player benefits by misreporting when the rest of the execution is frozen.

proof_stages.label: Application tests: voting and one-sided matching
proof_stages.summary: The framework is tested on simple auctions, cardinal voting, and one-sided matching, yielding a VCG-price characterization related to Hylland-Zeckhauser equilibria and showing why regularization is needed.

proof_stages.label: Scope limits and research directions
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion identifies conceptual obstacles for two-sided matching, possible online and machine-learning extensions, and open questions about direct optimization and weaker equilibrium notions when the learning dynamics do not converge.
